title: Finding: AGENTS.md and triage.md Rule 3 disagree on whether bd dep add <epic> <non-epic> works
description:
## Why

Surfaced while closing the AGENTS.md epic-gating correction and the triage.md
Rule 3 blind-spot warning. The two agent-facing sources make APPARENTLY
opposite claims about the SAME bd command:

- AGENTS.md (Attaching Issues to Epics): `bd dep add <epic> <non-epic>` is
  rejected with 'epics can only block other epics' — i.e. a non-epic cannot
  block its epic. Stated as a BLANKET rule, no parent-child exception.
- triage.md Rule 3: to gate an epic, run `bd dep add <epic> <sub-task>`
  (sub-task blocks epic). A sub-task is normally a non-epic, so this is the
  same operation AGENTS.md says is rejected.

The bd error string is itself suspect: 'epics can only block other epics'
describes an EPIC as the blocker, but in `bd dep add <epic> <non-epic>` the
blocker is the NON-EPIC (bd dep add <A> <B> = B blocks A). Message and command
do not line up, so the real v1.0.4 behaviour is unverified. The semantics
spike tested --parent for epics and bd dep add only for generic
<blocked>/<blocker> and sibling pairs; it never recorded the
epic-as-dependent bd dep add case.

## Candidate resolutions (verify, do not assume)

1. PARENT-CHILD EXCEPTION (lead hypothesis): a non-epic CAN block an epic via
   bd dep add IF it is already a --parent child of that epic. This reconciles
   both sources: Rule 3 works precisely because the sub-task is a --parent
   child. If true, AGENTS.md's blanket rejection is incomplete AND the
   recommended gating path IS the cycle-detector blind-spot path (--parent
   edge + antiparallel bd dep add) — making the triage.md warning central,
   not edge-case.
2. AGENTS.md correct, Rule 3 wrong: epic dep add always rejected; Rule 3's
   command fails for non-epic sub-tasks; epics genuinely cannot be gated.
3. Rule 3 correct, AGENTS.md wrong: dep add onto an epic succeeds; the
   rejection claim (and the just-landed AGENTS.md rewrite) is wrong.

## What

Verify against bd v1.0.4 in an ISOLATED sandbox repo (do NOT mutate the live
DB — see the auto-import data-loss workaround). Test, in one sandbox:
- bare `bd dep add <epic> <non-epic>` (no parent edge) — succeed or reject?
- same after `--parent <epic>` on the non-epic — does the parent edge change
  the outcome? (tests hypothesis 1)
- whether `bd dep cycles` flags the resulting antiparallel pair.
Then make AGENTS.md and triage.md agree, and record the epic-as-dependent
row in the spike table in triage.md.

## Ordering

Blocks the backlog wiring migration: the migration picks a canonical gating
mechanism and must not standardise on a command whose behaviour is unverified.
close_reason: VERIFIED in isolated bd v1.0.4 sandbox: bd dep add connects same-type only. epic<->task rejected both directions ('epics can only block other epics, not tasks' / 'tasks can only block other tasks'); --parent does NOT create exception (tested with and without parent edge). Resolution: hypothesis 1 (parent-child exception) FALSE; AGENTS.md correct; triage.md Rule 3 was wrong and is now corrected to state same-type constraint + spike-table row added. Docs agree.

title: Correct AGENTS.md epic-gating section to match validated --parent semantics
description:
## Why

AGENTS.md 'Epic Gating and Attaching Issues to Epics' (lines ~219-229)
falsely claims that `--parent` GATES the epic: 'the epic is excluded from
`bd ready` while any open child exists.' The validated spike
ansible-all-my-things-8p1t (bd v1.0.4, recorded in
docs/architecture/concepts/issue-tracking/triage.md) proved the opposite:
`--parent` is display-only and NEVER gates readiness — tested for epic,
feature, and task, all 'Yes — not gated'.

AGENTS.md is the agent runtime authority (CLAUDE.md mandates reading it before
any action). An agent trusting the false claim relies on epic-gating that never
happens — the exact mis-triage the spike was created to eliminate.

The section also states a REAL bd constraint (line 221): `bd dep add <epic>
<non-epic>` is rejected ('epics can only block other epics'). Combined with the
spike truth, the correct conclusion is: an epic CANNOT be gated out of
`bd ready` at all — `bd dep add` is rejected AND `--parent` does not gate.
An epic with open children always remains in `bd ready`.

## What

Rewrite the 'Epic Gating and Attaching Issues to Epics' section to:
- State `--parent` attaches a child for display/scope only; it does NOT gate
  readiness and does NOT exclude the parent from `bd ready`.
- Keep the real constraint: `bd dep add <epic> <non-epic>` is rejected.
- Conclude: an epic with open children REMAINS in `bd ready`; rely on its
  CHILDREN section / `bd show` for scope, not on `bd ready` exclusion.
- Cross-reference docs/architecture/concepts/issue-tracking/triage.md.

No re-verification needed — bd version unchanged (v1.0.4); spike evidence stands.

## Ordering

runtime guidance so no agent acts on the false gating claim mid-flight.

## Acceptance criteria

- AGENTS.md no longer claims `--parent` gates or excludes the parent from
  `bd ready`
- Section states the epic-cannot-be-gated truth and the non-epic-block rejection
- Section cross-references triage.md

  stays in `bd ready`, not that it is excluded

title: Cross-reference cycle-detector blind spot into triage.md Rule 3
description:
## Why

triage.md Rule 3 recommends `bd dep add <parent> <child>` (e.g.
`bd dep add <epic> <sub-task>`) to gate a container. Because a child created
with `--parent` already carries a `--parent` edge to that container,
following Rule 3 places a `bd dep add` edge on a pair that already has a
`--parent` edge — exactly the case the doc's own caveat (lines ~39-41) says
`bd dep cycles` cannot detect. Rule 3 currently leads a reader into that blind
spot without linking to the caveat; the two sections do not reference each other.

## What

Add a short warning to triage.md Rule 3 that the container-gating pattern
triggers the cycle-detector blind spot, with a pointer to the existing caveat
and the manual audit (`bd dep list <id> --direction up|down`). Reference the
bulk-migration stance from ansible-all-my-things-c8ya (avoid container-gating in
bulk; manual audit for deliberate one-offs). Additive clarification only — do
NOT reverse Rule 3's mechanics.

## Ordering — docs-first (blocks c8ya)

This BLOCKS ansible-all-my-things-c8ya. The migration must execute only after
triage.md no longer recommends the blind-spot edge; otherwise an agent could
follow Rule 3 and re-introduce a bad edge in the window between migration
completion and this fix. The rule documented here is already validated
(bd v1.0.4) and does not depend on the migration outcome, so it is safe to land
first.

## Acceptance criteria

- triage.md Rule 3 carries a visible warning linking to the blind-spot caveat
- Warning names the manual audit command and the c8ya bulk stance
- Rule 3's existing mechanics unchanged (no reversal)
- Cold-read: a fresh agent gating a container is steered to the manual audit,
  not silently into the blind spot
close_reason: triage.md Rule 3 warns of cycle-detector blind spot, links caveat, names manual audit cmd, inlines bulk-avoidance stance

title: Audit and migrate backlog wiring to canonical bd dep model
description:
## Why

Spike ansible-all-my-things-8p1t (bd v1.0.4) established that `--parent`
is display-only: it does NOT gate parents or children in `bd ready`. Only
`bd dep add` creates true blocking. The backlog contains ~45 open issues
across ~36 containers that may rely on `--parent` for implied ordering, while
none of those ordering expectations are enforced. The spike also found at
least one EXISTING antiparallel parent-child dep edge (on the RTK tree) that
`bd dep cycles` could not see.

NOTE ON 'CONTAINER': the affected unit is ANY issue that has children via
`--parent` — epic, feature, story, or task. The spike's own case was a
feature -> story -> task chain (ij9c -> 20ha -> 0oa6/7mfx), no epic involved.
'Container' below means any such parent, not only epics.

Goal: a SAFE, reliable reduction of wrong wiring — improve triage reliability
without introducing (or leaving) wiring the cycle detector cannot see.

## Core invariant

`bd dep cycles` does NOT detect a cycle formed when a `--parent` edge and a
`bd dep add` edge coexist between the SAME two issues (triage.md caveat,
empirically observed in the spike). The safe property is therefore:

  **Never let a dep edge and a `--parent` edge join the same pair of issues.**

This is stricter than 'siblings only': a dep edge is safe between ANY two
issues that do not already share a `--parent` edge (sibling pairs AND
legitimate cross-container ordering both qualify). It is unsafe between a
container and its own direct child — even though triage.md Rule 3 endorses
`bd dep add <parent> <child>` for container-gating.

## Reconciliation with triage.md Rule 3

Rule 3 recommends `bd dep add <parent> <child>` (child blocks its container)
to gate a container. This bulk migration DEFERS that pattern rather than
applying it, because it places a dep edge on a pair that already carries a
`--parent` edge — exactly the cycle-detector blind spot. The traded-away value
is cosmetic: a container sitting in `bd ready` is a benign tracking unit, not
actionable work. If a specific container genuinely needs gating later, do it as
a deliberate one-off WITH a manual `bd dep list <id> --direction up|down`
antiparallel audit — never in this bulk pass.

DOCS-FIRST: two guidance fixes BLOCK this migration so it executes only under
corrected docs — ansible-all-my-things-ug6q fixes triage.md Rule 3, and
ansible-all-my-things-0mhr corrects the false epic-gating claim in AGENTS.md.

## What

0. SNAPSHOT first: copy `.beads/issues.jsonl` so the whole migration is
   reversible.

1. REMEDIATE existing unsafe edges (highest-value step). Enumerate every
   `bd dep add` edge (from `.beads/issues.jsonl` or per-issue
   `bd dep list`) and every parent-child pair (issue `parent` fields).
   For any pair present in BOTH sets, the dep edge is in the blind spot:
   `bd dep rm <blocked> <blocker>` to remove it (the `--parent` hierarchy
   stays for display). Re-confirm intended ordering, if any, is re-expressed
   safely per step 2. (Note: such edges can exist only on NON-epic containers —
   bd rejects `bd dep add <epic> <non-epic>`.)

2. ADD ordering edges ONLY between pairs that do NOT share a `--parent` edge.
   Confirm the pair via `bd show` / parent fields before adding. Typical case
   is true siblings (same parent): `bd dep add <later> <earlier>`.
   Cross-container sequential deps are also permitted under the same invariant.
   Do NOT invent ordering: add a dep ONLY where a concrete data/artifact
   dependency exists; otherwise leave the issues flat.

3. Do NOT add `bd dep add <parent> <child>` edges (see Reconciliation above).
   Accept that containers remain in `bd ready` throughout — current, safe
   behavior.

4. VERIFY in batches. After each batch run `bd ready`, `bd blocked`,
   `bd dep cycles`. Because steps 2-3 keep every dep edge off any `--parent`
   pair, all dep-edge cycles (any hop count) live in detector-covered territory
   and `bd dep cycles` is authoritative — no blind spot remains. `--parent`
   is display-only and cannot route a readiness cycle.

## Execution & tracking — implementing agent decides

This stays a SINGLE issue. The implementing agent analyzes the actual backlog
shape first, then chooses how to batch and track the migrated containers
(e.g. by sub-tree, by container type, by risk) and what evidence to record.
Do NOT pre-split into sub-issues up front; split a batch out only if it proves
genuinely large or must pause mid-way. The step-0 snapshot is the rollback.

## Verification recipe (for the disjointness AC)

- Dep edges: parse `bd dep add` relationships from `.beads/issues.jsonl`
  (or loop `bd dep list <id>` over all open issues).
- Parent-child pairs: filter `dependencies` array for entries with `type: 'parent-child'` from `.beads/issues.jsonl`.
- Assert the two edge sets are DISJOINT (no shared {a,b} pair). This is
  O(edges), not O(pairs) — scriptable, not a manual 45-issue sweep.

## Scope

All open issues. Closed issues: skip (no operational impact). Only dep edges
that satisfy the core invariant are added; pre-existing blind-spot edges are
removed.

## Re-validation

Pinned to bd v1.0.4. If bd upgrades and its changelog touches parent-child
gating, hierarchy/readiness, or `bd ready`, re-check whether the blind spot
still exists before relying on this approach (see triage.md header).

## Audit outcome (2026-06-06)

Audit completed against 36 active issues (34 open, 2 in_progress). Result:
`blocks ∩ parent-child = ∅` — 0 blind-spot edges found (all 34 parent-child
pairs are between closed issues; 0 active parent-child pairs). 0 dep mutations
required.

Epic `ansible-all-my-things-0zd` closure: 13/13 children closed (100% complete,
eligible for close). Scoped as backlog hygiene discovered during audit scan;
closed in same session as c8ya.

## Acceptance criteria

- All pre-existing parent-child `bd dep add` edges removed (blind-spot edges
  eliminated)
- Ordering enforced only via dep edges between non-`--parent` pairs; zero
  `bd dep add <parent> <child>` edges remain or are added
- Dep-edge set and parent-child-pair set are DISJOINT (verified by the recipe)
- `bd dep cycles` returns no cycles after migration
- `bd ready` and `bd blocked` reflect intended work order
- Pre-migration `.beads/issues.jsonl` snapshot retained; post-migration
  `bd export` + `bd dolt push` completed
close_reason: Audit: 0 blind-spot edges (blocks ∩ parent-child = ∅, verified from JSONL dependencies array). 0 dep mutations needed. Recipe corrected. 0zd scoped as backlog hygiene.

title: Review PR and collect findings

Human reviews the PR and files each finding as a child task.

   bd create --title="<finding>" --type=task --parent <this-story-id>

All finding tasks must be closed before the merge task becomes actionable.

title: Spike: characterize bd/bv dependency & readiness semantics for next-action determination (v1.0.4)
description:
## Workspace

- Worktree: `.claude/worktrees/spike+8p1t-bd-bv-semantics`
- Branch: `worktree-spike+8p1t-bd-bv-semantics`
- Docs: `docs/beads-experiment/`

## Why

Two fresh Sonnet agents read the same RTK feature/container/findings hierarchy and drew OPPOSITE next-action conclusions (top-down vs leaves-up); neither matched 'bd ready'. Parent-child blocking semantics are ambiguous in beads v1.0.4, and a cycle formed from antiparallel parent-child + explicit edges was invisible to 'bd dep cycles'. Before re-wiring the backlog we need a formally verified ground truth.

## What

Run a time-boxed spike in an ISOLATED sandbox repo to characterize, with command evidence, the canonical wiring + canonical bd/bv command sequence that makes the 'what is the next immediate action' question unambiguous (autonomous-parallel / needs-human / recommended), then propagate learnings to the skill and the live RTK tree.

## Exit criteria

## Constraints

- Experiment runs in a SEPARATE git+beads repo; live RTK issues untouched
- One variable changed at a time
- Teardown restores known state

title: Replace RTK file-existence guard with binary installation check
description:
## Why

Current rtk-settings.yml checks whether RTK config/db files exist before backing up. This silently skips backup if files are unexpectedly missing (corruption, wrong user), violating Principle XII. It can also produce partial archives.

## What

Replace the stat-based file-existence guard with a binary installation check (which rtk). If RTK is not installed, skip the entire play gracefully. If RTK is installed but files are missing, fail loud with an explicit error message.

## Acceptance criteria

- rtk-settings.yml uses 'which rtk' (or ansible.builtin.command with failed_when) to detect installation
- RTK not installed: play skips, no error
- RTK installed, files missing: play fails with actionable error message
- Molecule test passes
close_reason: Implemented in refactor commit: binary installation check + Python tomllib resolver + pytest suite. Playbook hardened.

## What

## Acceptance criteria

- scripts/backup/resolve_rtk_db_path.py exists, parses TOML correctly via tomllib
- pytest suite covers: default path fallback, custom path under home, path outside home (error), path under ~/.config (error), missing config.toml (fallback), unquoted/multiline TOML values
- playbooks/backup/rtk-settings.yml uses script: module instead of inline shell

title: Back up and restore RTK settings
description:
## Why

RTK gain analytics history and config are lost on machine rebuilds. Need to preserve them via the existing backup/restore system.

## What

Add playbooks/backup/rtk-settings.yml and playbooks/restore/rtk-settings.yml, wired into the top-level backup.yml and restore.yml.

## Files to back up

- ~/.config/rtk/config.toml — RTK configuration (may contain custom database_path)
- Resolved DB path — default ~/.local/share/rtk/history.db, or the path set under [tracking].database_path in the config

## Backup behaviour

1. Shell task on remote parses database_path from ~/.config/rtk/config.toml; falls back to ~/.local/share/rtk/history.db if absent.
2. Fails loud if resolved path is outside the user's home directory (unsupported).
3. Archives both files with paths relative to home into rtk-backup.tar.gz via the shared backup.yml task.

## Restore behaviour

1. Extracts rtk-backup.tar.gz to home preserving relative paths (destination_beneath_home: "").
2. Deletes before extracting: .config/rtk/config.toml and .local/share/rtk/history.db (default path only — no attempt to clean up stale custom-path DBs).

## Wiring

- Add entry to top-level backup.yml and restore.yml.
- Same host/tag pattern as existing entries: not-supported-on-vagrant-docker, hosts: {{ backup_from_host }} for backup / hosts: linux for restore.

## Out of scope

- DB paths outside home directory.
- Merging history databases.
- Backing up tee debug output files.

## Acceptance criteria

- molecule test or manual test shows backup creates rtk-backup.tar.gz containing both files
- restore extracts files to correct locations
- dynamic DB path resolution works: custom path under home is backed up/restored; path outside home fails with explicit error
- top-level backup.yml and restore.yml include the new playbooks

## Status note (2026-06-04)

PR #64 merged to upstream. Manual test on real machine ongoing — keeping in_progress until results confirmed.
close_reason: Work done. PR #64 merged to upstream. Manual test confirmed working.

title: Verify CLAUDE.md survives omc setup on fresh machine
description:
## Goal
Confirm that running Ansible provision (rtk init -g) followed by interactive omc setup on a machine with no pre-existing CLAUDE.md produces a CLAUDE.md containing both the OMC block and the @RTK.md reference in the user section.

## Why
s0jt proposes removing ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md from the Claude settings backup because rtk init -g (run by Ansible provision) and omc setup together should fully regenerate it. If omc setup overwrites the file fresh rather than updating in-place, the @RTK.md reference written by rtk init -g would be lost. This test validates the assumption before CLAUDE.md is dropped from backup.

## Steps
1. Provision a fresh VM or container — no existing ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md
2. Run Ansible provision (rtk init -g fires in configure.yml)
3. Run omc setup interactively
4. Inspect ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md — must contain both the OMC block and @RTK.md in the user section, matching structure of the working dev machine CLAUDE.md

## Pass criteria
~/.claude/CLAUDE.md contains both the OMC markers block and the @RTK.md reference after steps 2+3.

## Fail criteria and remediation
If omc setup wipes the user section: either reorder provision steps (document manual re-run of rtk init -g after omc setup) or keep CLAUDE.md in the backup permanently.

## Output required
Report result (pass/fail) plus the actual CLAUDE.md content after the test, so s0jt can decide whether to drop CLAUDE.md from backup.
notes: PASS — verified on fresh VM (hobbiton, main branch). Steps: (1) rm ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, (2) rtk init -g → writes @RTK.md only, (3) omc setup interactively. Result: OMC block at top, @RTK.md preserved under user customizations section. Label variant was '(migrated from previous CLAUDE.md)' due to OMC v4.13.4 on VM vs 4.14.4 on dev — cosmetic only, collapses on next omc update. @RTK.md count=1, position after OMC:END. CLAUDE.md can be dropped from backup.
close_reason: PASS: @RTK.md survives omc setup. rtk init -g (Ansible) → @RTK.md only; omc setup merges it into user customizations section. CLAUDE.md safe to drop from backup.

title: Narrow claude backup/restore to role-unmanaged state after kwrv ships
description:
## Goal
After kwrv ships (skills become role-managed via the ai-agent-workspace clone; settings.json confirmed role-managed by configure.yml), narrow the claude backup to cover only genuinely irreplaceable user state: per-project memories and keybindings.

## Why
Pre-kwrv, backup was the only protection for CLAUDE.md, skills, and settings. Post-kwrv the role manages skills — continuing to back them up adds noise, risks restoring stale role-managed content over fresh role output, and obscures what backup is actually protecting.
The backup's purpose shifts from 'preserve installed tooling' to 'preserve user state that no role can recreate'.

## What is irreplaceable (keep in backup)
- ~/.claude/projects/ — per-project accumulated memories. Not reproducible from any role.
- ~/.claude/keybindings.json — custom keybindings.

## What to remove from backup and why

**~/.claude/skills/** — symlinks managed by kwrv (ai-agent-workspace clone + skills script). Safe to drop once kwrv ships.

**~/.claude/settings.json** — merged by configure.yml; omc-setup keys are re-added manually post-restore anyway.

**~/.claude.json** — not role-managed but user re-authenticates via 'claude login'; low cost to recreate.

**~/.claude/RTK.md** — regenerated by `rtk init -g` which already runs in configure.yml (step 2 of machine setup sequence). Safe to drop.

## Open question: CLAUDE.md on a fresh machine (must resolve before removing from backup)

Ideal sequencing is: `omc setup` first (establishes OMC markers), then `rtk init -g` (adds `@RTK.md` to the user section, which OMC never rewrites). But the current machine setup sequence has them reversed: `rtk init -g` runs in Ansible provision (step 2) before `omc setup` (step 4, interactive).

Risk: on a fresh machine with no CLAUDE.md, `rtk init -g` creates the file. Then `omc setup` runs — if it writes the file fresh rather than updating in-place, it wipes the `@RTK.md` reference. Result: fresh machine CLAUDE.md lacks RTK config and does not match the working dev machine CLAUDE.md.

**Required verification before removing ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md from backup:**
Test on a fresh VM/container (no existing CLAUDE.md):
1. Run Ansible provision (rtk init -g fires in configure.yml)
2. Run omc setup interactively
3. Inspect ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md — must contain both the OMC block and `@RTK.md` in the user section, matching the structure of the working dev machine CLAUDE.md.

If the test passes (OMC preserves user section on a file without prior markers): remove CLAUDE.md from backup.
If the test fails: either reorder the steps (document a manual re-run of `rtk init -g` after `omc setup`) or keep CLAUDE.md in the backup.

Until this is verified, **~/.claude/CLAUDE.md stays in the backup**.

## Changes to playbooks/backup/claude-settings.yml
Replace current broad path list with:
  path:
    - /home/{{ backup_user }}/.claude/projects
    - /home/{{ backup_user }}/.claude/keybindings.json
    # Add ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md here only after the open question above is resolved

Drop ~/.claude.json from path entirely.

Trim exclusion_patterns to only what applies to the narrowed paths:
  exclusion_patterns:
    - '*sessions*'
    - '*.omc/state*'
    - '*.omc/logs*'
    - '*cache*'

## Changes to playbooks/restore/claude-settings.yml
Narrow delete_before_beneath_home to match:
  delete_before_beneath_home:
    - .claude/projects
    - .claude/keybindings.json

Do NOT delete all of ~/.claude before restore — settings merges and other role-managed content must survive untouched.

## Machine setup sequence (document in docs/user-manual/)
Mandatory order for a fresh machine:
  1. restore.yml           — recovers memories + keybindings
  2. Ansible provisioning  — installs all role-managed content (includes rtk init -g)
  3. claude login          — interactive auth, cannot be automated
  4. omc setup             — interactive OMC config, cannot be automated
  4b. rtk init -g          — re-run manually if step 4 wiped the user section (pending verification above)

Steps 1+2 can be scripted; 3+4 are always manual. This order must be documented before the epic is closed.

## Gate
kwrv must be fully shipped and verified before implementing the skills/ removal from backup.
Resolve the CLAUDE.md open question (see above) before removing CLAUDE.md from backup.

## Acceptance criteria
- claude-settings backup only archives .claude/projects/, keybindings.json, and (pending verification) .claude/CLAUDE.md
- restore only deletes and restores those same paths (not all of ~/.claude)
- Machine setup sequence documented in docs/user-manual/ including the rtk init -g / omc setup ordering note
- A restore followed by provision produces a working Claude Code environment with no manual steps beyond login and omc setup
- Fresh VM CLAUDE.md structure matches working dev machine CLAUDE.md (OMC block + @RTK.md in user section)
close_reason: Closed

title: Parallel agents miss uncommitted main-worktree changes; worktree branches not cleaned up

During 6z2d/r1c parallel execution, uncommitted changes in main worktree (vars.yml, vault-template.yml) were invisible to the r1c agent working in an isolated worktree. Git worktree isolation is correct behavior, but the parallel-work workflow gave no warning. Two remediation actions needed:

title: Migrate all molecule scenarios to Dockerfile-based bootstrap
description:
All roles using raw apt bootstrap tasks in prepare.yml are vulnerable to rootless podman killing the container during Ansible's pre-Python file copy phase (podman cp --pause=false race). The claude_code role fixed this by pre-baking python3+sudo into a Dockerfile image instead of using raw tasks.

Migrate all remaining roles (dolt_sql_server, java, podman, python, ruby, tmux) to the same pattern:
1. Add minimal Dockerfile (python3+sudo only) to each role's molecule/default/
2. Switch molecule.yml to pre_build_image: false + dockerfile: Dockerfile + ANSIBLE_PODMAN_MOUNT_DETECTION: false
3. Remove raw bootstrap tasks from prepare.yml; replace role-specific extras (git, fontconfig, etc.) with normal apt module tasks
4. Update role-template/molecule/default/ to the new canonical form
5. Update molecule-testing skill SKILL.md to document new canonical Dockerfile, molecule.yml, and prepare.yml

Driving constraint: rootless podman on Ubuntu kills bare containers during the Ansible pre-Python bootstrap copy. Pre-baking Python into the image eliminates the copy step entirely.
close_reason: Closed

title: molecule test for claude_code role fails at converge: Claude Code installer checksum verification fails in arm64 container
description:
## Symptom
molecule test for claude_code role fails at converge with 'Checksum verification failed' from install.sh when running in arm64 Ubuntu 24.04 Podman container.

## Investigation findings
- Both CDN endpoints (downloads.claude.ai and GCS) serve identical manifests — checksums match
- Binary downloaded from CDN directly passes checksum: 98807675a3ed5b7b775f7eaa81eda32cba2810b97e9db9f6f98d7bd658cec00e (linux-arm64 v2.1.158)
- install.sh correctly detects aarch64 → linux-arm64 (not musl, ubuntu:24.04 is glibc)
- Failure is container-specific: most likely network unreliability inside Podman causing partial download of the ~50MB binary → SHA256 mismatch

## Fix options
A. Pre-stage a stub binary in prepare.yml so stat check finds claude already installed and role skips installer. Stub must pass Ansible integrity check OR role needs a molecule skip variable.
B. Add molecule-specific variable claude_code_skip_binary_install: false in converge.yml, guard install steps with when: not claude_code_skip_binary_install, stage a stub binary in prepare.yml.
C. Download and stage the real binary in prepare.yml (before converge), eliminating the in-converge download. Role detects it already present and skips installer.

## Preferred approach (TBD)
User decision needed: stub vs real binary in prepare, and whether to add skip variable.
close_reason: Resolved by Dockerfile-based molecule container (cbj commit 404b04c). Container bootstrap moved to Dockerfile, eliminating raw apt installs that caused container crashes. Full converge including Claude Code installer passed.

title: Configure Exa MCP server in claude_code role
description:
## What to configure
Add exa MCP server at user scope for each desktop user in roles/claude_code/tasks/.

## Technical details
Current state on dev machine: exa is configured per-project (found under projects.<path>.mcpServers in ~/.claude.json). Target state: user scope so it applies globally across all projects.

Install command (run as desktop user):
  claude mcp add --scope user exa --command npx -- -y exa-mcp-server

With environment variable for API key:
  claude mcp add --scope user exa --command npx --env EXA_API_KEY=<key> -- -y exa-mcp-server

Or configure via direct JSON manipulation of ~/.claude.json under a mcpServers key at user scope (if claude mcp add does not support --scope user directly — verify at implementation time).

## API key
Must come from Ansible Vault. Variable name: exa_api_key (to be confirmed at spec time).
Store in group_vars or host_vars encrypted with ansible-vault.
Never hardcode or log.

## Idempotency
Check if exa already present in claude mcp list output before adding.
Guard with: claude mcp list | grep -q '^exa:' or equivalent.

## MCP server config (for reference)
Type: stdio
Command: npx
Args: [-y, exa-mcp-server]
Env: EXA_API_KEY: <from vault>

## Independent
No dependency on F1, F2, or F3. Can be implemented in any order.

title: Fix idempotent plugin installation in claude_code role
description:
## Problem
Existing plugin install tasks in roles/claude_code/tasks/install-addons.yml (lines 81-119) use changed_when: false with no prior existence check. They re-run and potentially re-install on every provision run, violating Principle I.

Affected tasks (4 total: 2 marketplace-add + 2 plugin-install):
- Line 81: claude plugin marketplace add https://github.com/Yeachan-Heo/oh-my-claudecode
- Line 91: claude plugin install oh-my-claudecode
- Line 101: claude plugin marketplace add JuliusBrussee/caveman
- Line 111: claude plugin install caveman@caveman

## Required fix: probe → gate → mutate pattern (per user, per operation)

### Probe tasks (ONLY valid use of changed_when: false)
Run read-only CLI commands per user. changed_when: false + failed_when: false (same as omc_cli_check pattern at lines 57-66).

Probe for marketplaces:
  claude plugin marketplace list
  Output format: lines starting with '  ❯ marketplace-name'
  Whole-token match (not substring) for: 'omc' and 'caveman'

Probe for plugins:
  claude plugin list
  Output format: lines starting with '  ❯ name@marketplace'
  Whole-token match for: 'oh-my-claudecode@omc' and 'caveman@caveman'

### Gate tasks with when:
  when: (probe_result.results | selectattr('item', 'equalto', item) | first).stdout is not search('❯ omc')
Reuse the existing selectattr per-user pattern at line 75. Anchor the regex to avoid false positives.

### Mutating tasks
Remove changed_when: false. Run only when gate condition true. Report changed truthfully.

## Acceptance criteria
- Two probe registers: one for marketplace list, one for plugin list (both per user)
- All 4 mutating tasks gated by when: derived from probe
- changed_when: false present ONLY on probe tasks
- molecule idempotence step passes for claude_code role

## Context
- claude binary: /home/{{ item }}/.local/bin/claude
- Loop variable: desktop_user_names
- Probe pattern to reuse: install-addons.yml:57-66 (omc_cli_check)
- Per-user selectattr pattern to reuse: install-addons.yml:75
- Prerequisite for F3 (cbj) which adds more plugin tasks using same pattern
close_reason: probe→gate→mutate implemented; molecule idempotence passes (changed=0)

title: Reproducible Claude Code environment via Ansible
description:
## Goal
Provision a fully working Claude Code developer environment on a fresh Ubuntu machine via Ansible, without manual post-provision steps. Interactive `omc setup` is explicitly out of scope — it must remain manual.

## Current state
Role `roles/claude_code/` already handles:
- Claude Code binary install
- `oh-my-claude-sisyphus` npm package (OMC CLI) — note: npm package name differs from brand name
- `oh-my-claudecode` plugin (marketplace: https://github.com/Yeachan-Heo/oh-my-claudecode)
- `caveman` plugin (marketplace: JuliusBrussee/caveman)
- `bd` (beads issue tracker) and `bv` (beads viewer) CLI tools
- Source repo clones for omc and beads

## Missing / broken
- Plugin install tasks are not idempotent (changed_when: false, no existence check)
- `claude-plugins-official` marketplace (anthropics/claude-plugins-official) not added
- `context7` plugin not installed (auto-registers its MCP via bundled .mcp.json — no separate MCP config needed)
- Exa MCP server not configured (user scope, EXA_API_KEY from Vault)
- `specify-cli` (speckit) not installed — requires pipx
- No dedicated python role (pipx is the python-side dependency)
- Restore playbook has a broken `omc update` call that never worked reliably

## Architecture decisions
- Plugin idempotency: check existence before marketplace add and plugin install
- context7 MCP: auto-registered by plugin's bundled .mcp.json — no claude mcp add needed
- Exa MCP: `claude mcp add --scope user` with npx command, EXA_API_KEY from Vault
- Python tooling: use pipx (available via apt as python3-pipx), use community.general.pipx module for idempotent tool installs. uv rejected: requires curl-pipe-to-shell install, no apt package.
- speckit: install specify-cli via pipx pinned to v0.8.18 (latest as of 2026-05-31)
- python role: mirrors the ruby role pattern; claude_code role declares meta dependency on python role
- setup-desktop.yml installs python3-full — align by moving that responsibility into the python role

## Child features
F1: Idempotent plugin installation (fix existing)
F2: Python role
F3: context7 plugin
F4: Exa MCP server
F5: speckit CLI
F6: Retire broken restore step (gate: F1–F5 done)
close_reason: 13/13 children closed (100% complete, eligible for close).

title: Replace GitHub Releases path with Homebrew formula JSON for dolt version and checksum
description:
Currently, perform-updates.yml and query-versions.yml use fetch-github-release.yml (GitHub Releases API) to detect the latest dolt version. No checksum verification source exists independently of DoltHub.

Replace with https://formulae.brew.sh/api/formula/dolt.json as single source for both:
- stable.version → replaces GitHub latest-tag detection
- bottle.stable.files.x86_64_linux.sha256 and arm64_linux.sha256 → new dolt_checksum_amd64 / dolt_checksum_arm64 variables in defaults/main.yml
- get_url checksum: param in tasks/main.yml T007 uses these variables

Accepted trade-off: Homebrew lags DoltHub releases by hours-to-days. This is intentional — Homebrew acts as an independent trust anchor. GitHub-side tag relabeling or tarball tampering would not propagate until Homebrew independently verifies and publishes. Lag = supply chain defense.

Scope:
1. Add dolt_checksum_amd64 and dolt_checksum_arm64 to roles/dolt_sql_server/defaults/main.yml
2. Wire checksum: param in get_url task (tasks/main.yml)
3. Rewrite dolt section of perform-updates.yml to fetch Homebrew JSON, extract version + both checksums, update all three variables
4. Rewrite dolt section of query-versions.yml to fetch Homebrew JSON for version comparison
5. Remove or bypass fetch-github-release.yml for dolt (other roles using it are unaffected)
6. Update docs/architecture/version-update-playbooks.md tracked-tools table: source column → Homebrew formula API
close_reason: Not needed: Homebrew bottle sha256 is for ghcr.io OCI blob, not the GitHub tarball the role downloads. Checksums differ (verified live). Switching source would require full role rework with no practical benefit. Current GitHub-tarball approach is correct as-is.

title: New version-pinned tools must register with version-update playbooks
description:
## Why

Roles that pin an exact upstream version/checksum in defaults/main.yml must be tracked by playbooks/update-versions/ (see docs/architecture/version-update-playbooks.md). A new pinned tool that is NOT registered is invisible to query-versions.yml, so its pin silently drifts behind upstream security releases — the exact failure that design exists to prevent.

## What

Any future feature that adds a new pinned tool version (a *_version / *_identifier / *_build key plus optional checksum in a role defaults file) MUST also:
1. Register the tool in query-versions.yml (read pin, fetch upstream, report, extend fail-if-stale condition).
2. Register the tool in perform-updates.yml (fetch + ansible.builtin.replace the pin).
3. Add a row to the tracked-tools table in docs/architecture/version-update-playbooks.md.
4. Reuse an existing fetch task file where the upstream source type matches (GitHub Releases, Flutter JSON, SDKMAN, HTML scrape) — no duplicated fetch logic (FR-006 / Principle XI DRY). Add a new fetch task file only for a genuinely new source type.

## Scope

Policy/checklist item applied to all future role work. First instance: dolt_sql_server role (spec 008) registers dolt_version as a GitHub-release tool (dolthub/dolt).
acceptance_criteria: Every role defaults file that pins an upstream version is represented in both update-versions playbooks and in the tracked-tools table of version-update-playbooks.md; query-versions.yml reports the tool's drift status and fails when stale; fetch logic is reused per source type with no duplication.
notes: Trigger: spec 008 (dolt_sql_server) introduced dolt_version pin and surfaced the need for a standing policy. Source-type fetch files: fetch-github-release.yml (parametrized via github_repo), fetch-flutter-version.yml, fetch-java-version.yml, fetch-android-version.yml.

title: Auto-select best available VM size+datacenter combo in hcloud-linux provisioner
description:
## Problem
hcloud sometimes rejects the configured server_type+datacenter combination due to unavailability. Currently this causes a hard failure with no fallback.

## Goal
Automatically probe the prioritized list of size+location combinations and use the first one hcloud reports as available.

## Search sequence
Try in this exact order:
1. cx33 @ nbg1
2. cx33 @ fsn1
3. cx33 @ hel1
4. cx43 @ nbg1
5. cx43 @ fsn1
6. cx43 @ hel1

## Behavior
- First combo hcloud confirms available → use it for provisioning
- All 6 unavailable → hard fail with a clear error message listing what was tried
- No silent fallback, no interactive prompt

## Scope
provisioners/hcloud-linux.yml and its variable sources (inventories/group_vars/hcloud_linux/vars.yml)

title: Disable beads auto-backup git-push to silence git add warning
description:
## Problem

After any bd mutation (update/close/create), beads auto-backup fires and tries to git add .beads/issues.jsonl. The file is gitignored (.beads/ in .gitignore), so git refuses with a warning. This warning confuses AI agents into thinking something needs fixing.

## Fix

Set backup.git-push: false in .beads/config.yaml. Export still runs locally (beads viewer stays in sync), but no git add/push attempt is made. Remote sync continues via bd dolt push.

## Acceptance Criteria

- bd update produces no 'auto-export: git add failed' warning
- .beads/issues.jsonl is still written after mutations
- beads viewer stays in sync

title: Add beads backup/restore to backup.yml and restore.yml
description:
## Problem

The project's beads issue database (embedded Dolt DB) lives in `.beads/` inside the repository directory on the control machine. The directory is **partially gitignored** — `config.yaml` and `metadata.json` are tracked, but `issues.jsonl`, `backup/`, `dolt/`, `embeddeddolt/`, and `hooks/` are not. This means most of the database state is not in git and has no automated backup.

`bd export` and `.beads/issues.jsonl` are NOT a substitute — per the beads documentation:

> '`bd export` and `.beads/issues.jsonl` are issue-table exports. They are useful for review, migration, and interoperability, but they do not capture Dolt branches, commit history, working-set state, or non-issue tables.'
> 'Use `bd backup` or a manual Dolt backup when you need a restorable database backup.'

Source: https://github.com/gastownhall/beads

## Why this matters

Loss of `.beads/` (accidental deletion, disk failure, VM rebuild) means losing all issue history, dependency graph, and working-set state not captured in the jsonl export. There is no recovery path today.

## Verified recovery workflow

The following sequence has been manually verified to demonstrate that `bd backup` produces a fully restorable snapshot:

```bash
bd backup init ~/.beads/backup   # initialise backup destination
bd backup sync                   # push current DB state to backup
rm -rf .beads                    # simulate data loss
bd init                          # reinitialise beads (empty)
bd ready                         # → empty (confirms data is gone)
bd backup restore --force ~/.beads/backup
bd ready                         # → not empty (confirms full restore)
```

## Key assumptions — PRINT AND VERIFY before starting work

1. **Location**: `.beads/` is in the repository root on the control machine. Confirm `bd` commands run locally (not against a remote host).
2. **Backup path**: `~/.beads/backup` on the control machine — used in the verified workflow above. Confirm this is appropriate before hardcoding.
3. **Existing backup/restore structure**: the project already has `backup.yml` and `restore.yml` at the top level orchestrating sub-playbooks from `playbooks/backup/` and `playbooks/restore/`. Understand how this machinery works before proposing a solution.

## Time Machine reminder (not a requirement)

Consider whether periodic automatic backup (Apple Time Machine style, e.g. a scheduled `bd backup sync`) is worth adding alongside on-demand backup. Not an acceptance criterion — a reminder when scoping the solution.

title: Add scheduled workflow to detect new container-structure-test releases
description:
## Why

CST is pinned via curl in a run: step — Dependabot cannot track it. i3p pinned the version and SHA256 correctly but left no automated update signal. A new CST release will go unnoticed until someone manually checks.

## What needs to be done

Add a scheduled GitHub Actions workflow (e.g. .github/workflows/cst-version-check.yml) that:
1. Queries GitHub releases API for latest GoogleContainerTools/container-structure-test release
2. Compares against the pinned CST_VERSION in docker-publish.yml
3. Fails if a newer version exists

Schedule: weekly (e.g. Monday 05:00). Failure visible on default branch CI — does not block PRs.

## Acceptance criteria

- Scheduled workflow exists and runs weekly
- Workflow fails when CST_VERSION in docker-publish.yml is behind latest release
- Workflow passes when pinned version matches latest
- No impact on PR CI

title: Enable Group A zizmor audits (known-vulnerable-actions, hardcoded-container-credentials, unredacted-secrets)
description:
## Why

These three zizmor audits have near-zero false positive risk and catch real security bugs. They were disabled only to keep initial scope focused (YAGNI). No investigation needed before enabling.

## What needs to be done

In .github/zizmor.yml, remove disable: true for:
- known-vulnerable-actions
- hardcoded-container-credentials
- unredacted-secrets

Run zizmor locally to verify no existing findings. Fix any findings before committing.

## Acceptance criteria

- Three audits enabled in zizmor.yml
- pinning-lint CI passes with no new findings
- No regressions in existing workflow files

title: Expand zizmor audit coverage beyond pinning-only
description:
## Why

zizmor.yml currently disables all audits except unpinned-uses (YAGNI at initial scope). The project has grown: artifact-based builds, fork PR handling, elevated permissions jobs. Additional audits are now warranted.

## What needs to be done

Three tracks:
1. Enable Group A audits immediately (known-vulnerable-actions, hardcoded-container-credentials, unredacted-secrets)
2. Enable Group B audits after fork-safe-docker-ci merges (artipacked, cache-poisoning, excessive-permissions, template-injection, dangerous-triggers, impostor-commit)
3. Investigate Group C audits and decide per-item (self-hosted-runner, unpinned-images, unpinned-tools, use-trusted-publishing, job-container-escape)

## Acceptance criteria

- All zizmor audits have an explicit enable/disable decision with rationale documented
- No audit remains disabled purely by YAGNI default without re-evaluation

title: Document zizmor.yml allow-list requirement in ADR-002
description:
## Why

ADR-002 describes the two-tier pinning policy and the GitHub repo allow-list (Option G) but does not mention that zizmor.yml must also be updated when adding a new GitHub Action. Contributors hitting lint failures may fix the SHA pin but not know zizmor.yml is a separate gatekeeping mechanism.

## What needs to be done

- Add a 'Adding a new GitHub Action' checklist section to ADR-002 covering:
  1. Pin to SHA (Tier A) or use trusted exception (Tier B)
  2. Add to .github/zizmor.yml allow-list
  3. If forking: add to GitHub repo allow-list (Option G)
- Clarify the relationship between the three mechanisms and why each exists

## Acceptance criteria

- ADR-002 has a checklist section for adding a new GitHub Action
- All three gatekeeping mechanisms are documented with rationale
- No contributor should need to reverse-engineer zizmor.yml from a lint failure
close_reason: ADR-002 Adding-a-new-action procedure now documents pinning-lint CI and zizmor.yml wildcard coverage; no per-action entry needed

title: Finding 6: Update create-adr skill so mitigations are considered before the recommendation
description:
The create-adr skill (ai-agent-workspace/.claude/commands/create-adr.md) currently has these stages in order:
  Stage 4 — add common alternatives
  Stage 5 — mitigate negative consequences
  Stage 6 — verify decision drivers
  Stage 7 — complete the ADR draft
  Stage 8 — restructure for readability
  Stage 9 — final review

By the time mitigations are added (Stage 5), the recommendation has effectively already been made in Stage 3 (draft). Mitigations end up as per-option apologetics that do not feed back into the option-selection logic.

Symptom observed during ADR-002 review: critic finding C-6 (asymmetric option treatment — chosen option mitigated, rejected options loaded with unmitigated negatives) and the need for Finding 4 (re-evaluate recommendation against mitigations).

Fix the skill so the recommendation is provisional until after mitigations and decision drivers are integrated. Provisional re-ordering:

  Stage 3.5: provisional recommendation (draft, flagged as such)
  Stage 4: alternatives + mitigations for each, including for the provisional recommendation
  Stage 5: decision drivers (with priorities)
  Stage 6: re-evaluate the provisional recommendation against (a) all options' mitigations and (b) the priority-ranked drivers; finalize or shift recommendation
  Stage 7: consequences and follow-up
  Stage 8: restructure
  Stage 9: final review

Acceptance:
- create-adr skill updated with re-ordered stages
- Stage 6 explicitly requires re-walking the option matrix with mitigations included
- Skill committed via ai: prefix in the ai-agent-workspace repo (not this repo)

Scope note: this finding applies to a skill that lives outside this repository. Implementation is in ai-agent-workspace; tracking the finding here is appropriate because ADR-002 surfaced the gap and this repo's ADR practice will benefit.

title: Security: Disable Administrator RDP login
description:
## Why

Leaving Administrator RDP access enabled is a security risk — direct admin login over RDP is a common attack vector.

## What

Disable RDP login for the Administrator account during provisioning.

## Open Question

Need to determine how to perform GUI operations that require Administrator after disabling RDP for that account. Hypothesis: a normal user with admin group membership can re-enable Administrator RDP access via elevated command prompt when needed.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Administrator account cannot log in via RDP after provisioning
- A documented procedure exists for temporarily re-enabling Administrator RDP access when required for GUI operations
- Provisioning step is idempotent
notes:
## References

- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/windows-security/access-computer-after-administrator-disabled
- https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/manufacture/desktop/enable-and-disable-the-built-in-administrator-account?view=windows-11

## Key Finding

Enable/disable built-in Administrator account via elevated command prompt:

```cmd
net user administrator /active:yes
net user administrator /active:no
```

Normal user with admin privileges can run this to temporarily re-enable Administrator RDP access when needed for GUI operations.

title: Security: Install Windows updates during provisioning
description:
## Why

Freshly provisioned Windows instances may have unpatched vulnerabilities. Updates must be applied as early as provisioning allows to reduce attack surface.

## What

Add a provisioning step that installs all pending Windows updates on the host.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Windows updates installed automatically during provisioning
- Provisioning fails or warns if updates cannot be applied
- Step runs early enough in provisioning sequence to minimize exposure window

title: Windows: nvm not recognized after install — Node.js LTS install fails
description:
## Problem

Installing Node.js LTS via nvm fails on Windows. nvm is not recognized as a command despite being installed via Chocolatey.

## Error

Host: moria, task: Install Nodejs LTS

```
nvm : The term 'nvm' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or
operable program. Check the spelling of the name, or if a path was included, verify that the path
is correct and try again.
At line:1 char:65
+ ... le]::InputEncoding = New-Object Text.UTF8Encoding $false; nvm install ...
+                                                               ~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : ObjectNotFound: (nvm:String) [], CommandNotFoundException
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : CommandNotFoundException
```

rc: 1

## Root Cause (hypothesis)

nvm installed via Chocolatey but PATH not refreshed in the current Ansible session. Windows requires a new shell session or explicit PATH reload after Chocolatey installs.

## Workaround

Disable the entire role `win_ai_agent` — not needed for current scope.

## Acceptance Criteria

- nvm is recognized in the same Ansible session after install
- `nvm install lts` completes successfully without manual intervention

title: Windows Bug Fixes and Improvements
description:
## Purpose

Umbrella epic for all Windows provisioning bugs and improvements discovered during hands-on sessions.

## Scope

Tracks issues found while manually provisioning and using the Windows (moria) host on AWS, including bugs, workarounds, and follow-up documentation tasks.

title: Windows provisioning fails: Chocolatey install denied (Win32ErrorCode 5)
description:
## Problem

Windows provisioning fails when installing Chocolatey due to missing permissions.

## Error

Host: moria

```
- name: Install Chocolatey
     ^ column 3

fatal: [moria]: FAILED! => {
    "changed": false,
    "cmd": "Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force\n[System.Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol = [System.Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol -bor 3072\niex ((New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString('https://community.chocolatey.org/install.ps1'))",
    "msg": "Failed to run: '': Exception calling \"CreateProcess\" with \"7\" argument(s): \"CreateProcessW() failed (Access is denied, Win32ErrorCode 5 - 0x00000005)\"",
    "rc": 5
}
```

## Root Cause (hypothesis)

Ansible is not running with Administrator privileges on the Windows target. `CreateProcessW` Win32ErrorCode 5 = Access Denied — process spawning blocked by insufficient permissions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Chocolatey installs successfully on moria without manual intervention
- Role validates or ensures elevated privileges before running install script
notes:
## Manual Workaround (resolved)

### Steps
1. Log in as Administrator on the Windows machine and reboot
2. After reboot, retry provisioning:
   ```bash
   ansible-playbook ./provision.yml --extra-vars "provider=aws platform=windows" -vvv >provision-aws-windows.log 2>&1
   ```

### Outcome
Problem resolved after step 1 (reboot as Administrator).

### AWS Note: Checking server readiness after reboot
Ping does NOT work on AWS Windows machines. Use nmap with -Pn to skip host discovery:
```bash
sudo nmap -v -sS -Pn -p 3389 $IPV4_ADDRESS
```
Port 3389 (RDP) open = machine ready. This applies to AWS machines in general.

title: Evaluate version-update mechanism: separation, Python rewrite, or external tool
description:
**Problem:** Current Ansible implementation has test coverage gaps, gets complex when adding tools, is fragile, hard to test, hard to extend. Open questions on structural direction before further investment.

**Why:** Implementation getting complex and fragile. Risk wasting effort on Ansible-based fixes (ypm, 2fr, g0k) if a different foundation is chosen.

**Questions to evaluate (merged from 48h):**
1. Should version check/upgrade tasks move to tool-specific subfolders for separation of concerns?
2. Should the mechanism be rewritten in Python with a plugin architecture to make adding new tools easier?
3. Is Renovate or another existing dependency-update tool a better fit than custom Ansible playbooks?

**Value:** Picking the right foundation enables easier testing, clearer logic, simpler extension, better error handling.

**Decision required:** Pick direction (keep Ansible refactor / Python rewrite / external tool) before making Ansible-based changes that might become obsolete.

**Note:** This must be resolved before ypm, 2fr, g0k to avoid wasted effort. Merged from 48h on 2026-05-13.

title: Eliminate duplication between query-versions.yml and perform-updates.yml
description:
**Problem:** Duplication between playbooks/update-versions/query-versions.yml and perform-updates.yml causes changes needed in multiple files, risks inconsistency, and obscures source of truth.

**Why:** Future maintainability, contradicts 'no duplication' principle.

**Value:** Single source of truth, easier updates, clearer intent, easier to understand and less confusion.

**Solution:** Extract common patterns from both playbooks into shared components or interfaces.

title: Refactor playbooks/tasks/query-versions.yml for tool-based grouping with interface pattern
description:
**Problem:** Current phase-based grouping causes scattered logic and requires changes across multiple sections when adding new tools.

**Why:** Improving future maintainability.

**Value:** Tool-based grouping with interface definition enables single point of extension, applying Open/Closed Principle, high cohesion and loose coupling — concepts easier to understand and implement.

**Solution:** Regroup query-versions.yml by tool instead of phase. Each tool implements a common interface (read pins, extract pins, fetch upstream, compare and report, fail if stale). Encapsulate tool-specific steps as separate tasklists.

title: Extend new-role.sh for version-update pattern consideration
description:
**Problem:** New roles risk installing tools at outdated versions, introducing security risks.

**Why:** Improving consistency for future role creation.

**Value:** Avoid forgetting to consider version updates when creating new roles.

**Solution:** Extend new-role.sh script and templates to incorporate playbooks/update-versions concept so new roles bake in version-update tasks from creation.

title: Finding: constitution/role checklist must mandate registering new tools in version-update playbooks
description: When a new role is created that installs a versioned tool, there is currently no rule requiring the author to add that tool to the version-update mechanism (fetch task file, query-versions.yml, perform-updates.yml, concept doc). This gap means new tools silently escape version tracking. The constitution or a role-creation checklist must mandate: for every new role that pins a version in defaults/main.yml, a corresponding fetch task and playbook wiring must be added.
close_reason: Closed

title: Finding: document maintenance-playbook exception to Principle II in constitution
description: The justified exception allowing maintenance playbooks (procedural localhost operator tools) to contain inline logic rather than wrapping roles is only recorded in per-feature plan.md Complexity Tracking tables. It should be canonically stated in the constitution under Principle II so future agents apply it consistently. Name the pattern explicitly and reference playbooks/backup/, playbooks/restore/, playbooks/update-versions/ as precedents. Discuss exact wording when fixing.

title: Finding: SDKMAN query hardcoded to linuxx64, may miss arm64 availability
description: fetch-java-version.yml queries https://api.sdkman.io/2/candidates/java/linuxx64/versions/all. Temurin version strings are typically arch-agnostic in SDKMAN, but availability can diverge between amd64 and arm64. First step: verify whether a linuxarm64 endpoint exists in the SDKMAN API. If yes: query both and take intersection to find highest version available on all target architectures (amd64 hobbiton/rivendell, arm64 lorien). If no: keep current impl but document the limitation in docs/architecture/version-update-playbooks.md.

title: Finding: all tools need explicit major version pin
description: Only Java enforces same-major-patch tracking via java_sdkman_major. Flutter, gitmux, Nerd Fonts, and Android have no major version pin in role defaults. A major upstream bump passes silently through both playbooks. Each tool needs a pinned major variable in defaults and query-versions.yml should detect major-boundary crossings with a distinct error, separate from normal staleness.

title: Configure major version pinning for all tracked tools
description: Currently only Java pins major version explicitly (java_sdkman_major derived from java_sdkman_identifier). Other tools (Flutter, gitmux, Nerd Fonts, Android) have no major version pin, so a major version bump upstream would silently pass through query-versions.yml and perform-updates.yml without any warning. Need a mechanism to pin the expected major for each tool and detect when upstream crosses a major boundary, keeping major upgrades a deliberate manual decision.

title: Constitution: add Fail-Loud principle (no silent skip / no empty fact fallthrough)
description:
Elevate the fail-loud NFR (currently spec.md FR-012 in feature 007-version-update-playbooks) into a cross-cutting constitution principle.

Decision (2026-05-12): Failure modes that silently skip work, return empty values, or proceed with unset/None facts are prohibited. Any task that fetches, parses, or computes a required value MUST fail with an explicit error identifying the source when the value cannot be obtained.

Scope to think through:
- Wording of the principle (e.g. Principle VIII: Fail Loud)
- MUST/SHOULD scope: applies to all roles, playbooks, task files
- Relationship to existing Principle I (Idempotency): fail-loud is orthogonal but reinforces predictability
- Version bump: MINOR (new principle)
- Sync impact: AGENTS.md, CLAUDE.md, templates, possibly fix-problem skill
- Once added: spec.md FR-012 in feature 007 can reference the principle instead of restating; per-task notes in tasks.md may simplify

Trigger: surfaced during /speckit.analyze on feature 007-version-update-playbooks where 3 of 4 fetch tasks lacked explicit parse-failure handling. User decision: not a feature-local concern — belongs in constitution.

title: Molecule scenarios across roles share hardcoded podman container/image name 'instance', colliding under concurrent agent sessions
description:
## Context
While diagnosing ansible-all-my-things-aie9 (tmux molecule container dying
mid-converge), root cause was confirmed via journalctl: a concurrent agent
session running 'molecule test' for the claude_code role executed
'podman rm --force instance' and recreated a container also named 'instance'
at the exact moment another session's tmux scenario container was using that
same name. Every role's molecule/*/molecule.yml in this repo defines
`platforms: - name: instance` literally, with no per-role or per-scenario
uniqueness, and image name follows `molecule_<rolename>_instance` (already
unique) but the *container* name 'instance' itself is not. git worktrees
isolate the filesystem/git state between parallel agent sessions but do NOT
isolate the shared podman engine on one host, so two sessions running
molecule for different roles at the same time can destroy each other's
containers.

## Why not blocking
This is a pre-existing characteristic of the molecule-testing skill's
canonical template, not something introduced or required by the tmux fix.
The tmux fix (ansible-all-my-things-jqmx) was unblocked simply by serializing
the two sessions' molecule runs; no template change was needed to ship it.

## Possible directions (not prescribed)
- Give the container a per-scenario unique name baked from $$MOLECULE_SCENARIO_NAME$$ or repo path.
- Document the constraint (don't run molecule concurrently across sessions on one host).
- Accept as a known limitation and rely on user-level serialization.
acceptance_criteria: Either the molecule-testing skill template documents/mitigates the cross-session container-name collision, or the team explicitly accepts it as a known limitation with rationale recorded here.

title: Tart hostname pool selection ignores pool order (non-deterministic across invocations)
description: playbooks/tasks/create/tart.yml:73 uses (hostname_pool | difference(used_hostnames)) | first, same as the Docker provider (ansible-all-my-things-<new docker bug>). Ansible's difference filter is list(set(a)-set(b)) — order depends on Python's per-process hash seed, not pool order, contradicting the 'pick the first hostname not yet in use' comment and the Tart provider's intended sequential allocation (009-create-destroy-vm spec). Pre-existing bug, not introduced by 011-docker-vm-provider; out of scope for beads 9au2 (FR-017 requires tart.yml unchanged). Found incidentally during e2e test of beads 9au2.
acceptance_criteria: hostname selection in playbooks/tasks/create/tart.yml deterministically picks the first hostname_pool entry not present in current_inventory.all.hosts, in pool-file order
close_reason: Mirrored docker.yml fix (c2a467b) to tart.yml: reject('in',...)|list|first preserves pool order, syntax-checked for provider=tart.

title: Rename vagrant_tart inventory group and vars to tart
description:
The autogenerated inventory file and the inventory group written by the
direct-tart create/destroy flow still carry the vagrant_ prefix, which is
misleading now that vagrant was removed from this flow in 6r6j.

## Scope

title: Remove beads ID from triage.md attribution (Principle X: no tracker IDs in durable artefacts)
description:
## Why

triage.md ends with: *Derived from the bd/bv semantics spike
(`ansible-all-my-things-8p1t`).* triage.md is a durable Markdown artefact
under docs/architecture/. Principle X prohibits beads (or other ephemeral
tracker) IDs in durable artefacts — the substance must be inlined, not the ID.
The ID will dangle once the tracker is migrated or renumbered.

Surfaced while adding the Rule 3 blind-spot warning; left untouched then to
keep that change scoped.

## What

Replace the trailing ID citation with an ID-free attribution that inlines the
relevant substance (e.g. 'Derived from the bd/bv semantics spike validated
against bd v1.0.4.'). Keep the validation/version context; drop the bare
tracker ID.

title: Consolidate generic beads guidance into shared AGENTS.md; forget absorbed memories
description:
## Why

AGENTS.md is maintained as a similar/shared file across multiple of the user's
projects; bd memories are PER-PROJECT and do not propagate. Generic beads
knowledge currently lives only in this project's bd memories, so other projects
lack it — and some of it has drifted to contradict the validated spike
(ansible-all-my-things-8p1t). Consolidating the cross-project-true beads rules
into a portable AGENTS.md block (a) makes them available in every project via
the user's shared template, (b) creates a single source of truth that kills the
drift between triage.md / AGENTS.md / memories, and (c) lets the per-project
memories be forgotten so they stop fragmenting.

NOTE: 'shared' is MANUAL — editing this repo's AGENTS.md updates only this copy;
the cross-project win lands when the user promotes the block into the template.
Deliverable is therefore a clean, lift-and-drop block, not an auto-propagating
change.

## Scope — what moves to the portable AGENTS.md beads block

Generic, cross-project bd guidance. bd-behaviour-specific content VERSION-TAGGED
to bd v1.0.4:
- Parent/dep semantics: `--parent` is display-only (does NOT gate `bd ready`);
  only `bd dep add` blocks; epics cannot be blocked by non-epics
  (`bd dep add <epic> <non-epic>` is rejected); therefore a container with open
  children REMAINS in `bd ready`.
- Cycle-detector blind spot: never put a `bd dep add` edge on a pair already
  joined by `--parent`; `bd dep cycles` cannot see that antiparallel case;
  audit manually with `bd dep list <id> --direction up|down`.
- bd v1.0.4 auto-import data-loss workaround — CLEARLY gated 'bd 1.0.4 only':
  export after EVERY mutation, never bundle — `bd export --all -o
  .beads/issues.jsonl`. Removal is part of the eventual move to a bd release
  that fixes auto-import WITHOUT the 1.0.5 regression.
- Workflow conventions: findings always tied to WIP; `export.git-add: false`
  config note.

## What

1. Draft a concise, portable beads-guidance block for AGENTS.md (lean — a shared
   doc stays tight). Version-tag bd-specific content to 1.0.4.
2. Land it in this repo's AGENTS.md, building on the corrected baseline from
   0mhr/ug6q (do not duplicate or re-contradict).
3. `bd forget` the memories it absorbs:
   - after-any-beads-changes-bd-close-create-update
   - bd-1.0.4-autoimport-reverts-unexported-mutations
   - findings-are-always-connected-to-wip
   - if-you-see-warning-auto-export-git-add
   - beads-dep-direction-for-epics-sub-tasks-block — CORRECT, do NOT copy
     verbatim: it recommends `bd dep add epic sub-task`, which bd rejects for
     non-epics AND is the blind-spot pattern. Replace with the corrected
     semantics in the block, then forget.
4. LEAVE untouched, in-repo (do NOT put in the shared AGENTS.md — would pollute
   other projects): dolt_sql_server handler restart, per-arch-docker-image-tags,
   scripts-test-molecule-all-sh, verify-yml-async-smoke-test (project-specific
   role/CI facts; relocate to docs/ or keep as memories — separate decision).

## Ordering

Depends on (sequenced AFTER) ansible-all-my-things-0mhr and
ansible-all-my-things-ug6q: they reconcile the AGENTS.md epic-gating section and
triage.md Rule 3; this task consolidates onto that corrected baseline so the same
guidance is not edited twice or left contradictory. Does NOT block
ansible-all-my-things-c8ya.

## Acceptance criteria

- AGENTS.md carries a single, lean, portable beads-guidance block; all
  bd-version-specific content explicitly tagged 'bd 1.0.4'
- The 5 absorbed memories forgotten (no duplication/drift); the broken
  dep-direction memory corrected or removed
- The 4 role/CI memories untouched and remain in-repo
- Block is self-contained enough to lift into the cross-project AGENTS.md
  template
- No contradiction remains between AGENTS.md, triage.md, and bd memories on
  parent/dep semantics
notes: UPDATE 2026-06-05: beads-dep-direction-for-epics-sub-tasks-block was ALREADY forgotten preemptively (it actively mis-advised). So step 3 no longer needs to forget it — just ensure the CORRECTED dep/parent semantics land in the AGENTS.md block. The other 4 absorbed memories are still present and must be forgotten by this task.
close_reason: AGENTS.md carries portable beads-guidance block (data-safety, git-add config, findings-WIP rule). 4 absorbed memories forgotten; 4 project-specific memories untouched. No contradiction remains between AGENTS.md, triage.md, and memories on parent/dep semantics.

title: Add TD-010 to tech debt register: destination_beneath_home arcroot hack violates principle of least surprise
description:
Add a new entry TD-010 to docs/architecture/technical-debt/technical-debt.md.

## What to document
destination_beneath_home: '.claude/projects' in playbooks/restore/claude-settings.yml sets a value whose dirname is the actual extract destination — not the value itself. A developer reading this expects files to land under .claude/projects/; they actually land under .claude/. The parameter is being used as an arcroot anchor, not as a destination path.

## Root cause
Narrowing backup path to ~/.claude/projects + ~/.claude/keybindings.json shifts the tar arcroot from ~/ to ~/.claude/. The restore task computes dest = dirname(destination_beneath_home), so .claude/projects was chosen to make dirname equal .claude. No comment explains this.

## Fix options (for Ideas for solution section)
A. Narrow-by-exclusion: keep backup path: ~/.claude, exclude everything except projects/ and keybindings.json. Arcroot stays at ~/, destination_beneath_home: .claude reads correctly. Tradeoff: deny-list is fragile long-term.
B. Extend restore.yml to accept an explicit dest parameter, bypassing dirname computation.

## Category / Severity
Category: Technical Debt. Severity: Low (no correctness impact; only misleads future developers). Affected files: playbooks/backup/claude-settings.yml, playbooks/restore/claude-settings.yml, playbooks/restore/restore.yml.

title: Evaluate Renovate for automated tracking of pinned binary versions
description:
## Why

Binaries installed via curl in run: steps (e.g. container-structure-test) are invisible to Dependabot. Renovate supports a regex datasource that can match version strings in arbitrary files against upstream release feeds, enabling automated update PRs including SHA256 refresh.

## What needs to be done

- Evaluate Renovate regex datasource for tracking CST_VERSION and SHA256 env vars in docker-publish.yml
- Assess setup cost and maintenance overhead vs. the scheduled check workflow approach
- If viable, configure Renovate and retire the scheduled cst-version-check workflow

## Acceptance criteria

- Decision documented: adopt Renovate or explicitly keep scheduled check
- If adopted: Renovate opens PRs for CST updates with correct version and SHA256 values
- If rejected: rationale recorded here

title: Add rtk gain history to backup/restore playbooks
description:
## Why

rtk tracks token savings in ~/.local/share/rtk/history.db (SQLite, 336K). Without backup, gain history resets on every VM rebuild or machine switch.

## What needs to be done

Create two new playbooks and wire them into backup.yml / restore.yml, following the exact pattern of claude-settings.yml.

---

### 1. Create playbooks/backup/rtk-data.yml

```yaml
---
- name: Backup rtk data
  hosts: "{{ backup_from_host }}"
  tags: not-supported-on-vagrant-docker
  become: true

  vars:
    ansible_user: "{{ my_ansible_user }}"
    backup_user: "{{ desktop_users[0].name }}"

  tasks:
    - name: Backup rtk gain history
      include_tasks: backup.yml
      vars:
        path:
          - "/home/{{ backup_user }}/.local/share/rtk/history.db"
        backup_file: "rtk-backup.tar.gz"
```

### 2. Create playbooks/restore/rtk-data.yml

```yaml
---
- name: Restore rtk data
  hosts: linux
  tags: not-supported-on-vagrant-docker
  become: true

  vars:
    ansible_user: "{{ my_ansible_user }}"
    restore_user: "{{ desktop_users[0].name }}"

  tasks:
    - name: Restore rtk gain history
      include_tasks: restore.yml
      vars:
        users: "{{ [restore_user] }}"
        backup_file: "rtk-backup.tar.gz"
        destination_beneath_home: ".local/share/rtk"
        delete_before_beneath_home:
          - ".local/share/rtk/history.db"
```

### 3. Wire into backup.yml

Add to /home/galadriel/Documents/Cline/ansible-all-my-things/backup.yml under the Applications section:
```yaml
- import_playbook: playbooks/backup/rtk-data.yml
```

### 4. Wire into restore.yml

Add to /home/galadriel/Documents/Cline/ansible-all-my-things/restore.yml under the Applications section:
```yaml
- import_playbook: playbooks/restore/rtk-data.yml
```

---

## Key design decisions (do not deviate)

- Only history.db backed up — .device_salt intentionally excluded (machine-specific identity; restoring it corrupts telemetry fingerprinting on new hardware)
- delete_before_beneath_home scoped to history.db only — leaves .local/share/rtk/ dir intact so new machine's .device_salt survives the restore
- No exclusion_patterns needed — single file target
- Pattern source: playbooks/backup/claude-settings.yml and playbooks/restore/claude-settings.yml

## Reference files

- Pattern: playbooks/backup/claude-settings.yml, playbooks/restore/claude-settings.yml
- Shared tasks: playbooks/backup/backup.yml, playbooks/restore/restore.yml
- Entry points: backup.yml, restore.yml (project root)
acceptance_criteria:
- playbooks/backup/rtk-data.yml exists and matches the template above exactly
- playbooks/restore/rtk-data.yml exists and matches the template above exactly
- backup.yml imports rtk-data.yml under the Applications section
- restore.yml imports rtk-data.yml under the Applications section
- ansible-lint passes on all four modified/created files
- .device_salt is NOT in the backup path
- delete_before_beneath_home targets only history.db, not the whole rtk dir

title: Docs: AWS machines don't respond to ping — use nmap -Pn to check readiness
description:
## Why

AWS EC2 instances (including Windows) do not respond to ICMP ping. This trips up anyone waiting for a machine to be ready after boot or reboot.

Discovered while troubleshooting Windows provisioning (see ansible-all-my-things-dev Chocolatey bug).

## What to document

Add a note to the AWS docs explaining:
- Ping does NOT work on AWS machines
- Use nmap with -Pn (skip host discovery) to check reachability:
  ```shell
  # Windows (RDP port)
  sudo nmap -v -sS -Pn -p 3389 $IPV4_ADDRESS

  # Linux (SSH port)
  sudo nmap -v -sS -Pn -p 22 $IPV4_ADDRESS
  ```
- Port shown as 'open' = instance ready

## Where to add

- test/test_aws_provisioning.md — Step 1, after the provisioning commands (waiting for readiness context)
- docs/user-manual/prerequisites-aws.md — as operational tip

title: Parallelize molecule test runner: Python rewrite with concurrent execution and smoke test
description:
## Background

Per-role Molecule images cause CI pipeline regression: 12 min vs 8 min baseline. Root cause: Podman layer cache cold on every CI runner; sequential execution means 7 × 30–60s apt-get per role = 3–7 min overhead on every PR run.

## Agreed solution

### 1. Rewrite `scripts/test-molecule-all.sh` as Python

`scripts/test-molecule-all.py [--parallel N] [<role>]`

- `--parallel N`: max concurrent roles (default 4, RAM-aware)
- `<role>`: optional single-role mode for developer spot-checks
- Parallel execution via `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` + `as_completed()`
- Output buffered per role — printed on completion, no interleaving
- Collects exit codes per role; exits 1 if any fail

### 2. CI calls the script directly — no matrix

CI job calls `python scripts/test-molecule-all.py`. Developer runs same command locally — exact parity.

### 3. Integration smoke test

Minimal fixture role (`roles/_test_fixture/`) with trivial molecule scenario. Smoke test invokes `test-molecule-all.py` against the fixture and asserts exit 0. Acts as platform regression canary for Python and molecule version updates.

## Caching

Not included. After parallelism, image build overhead drops to ~2 min (parallel batches). Podman storage caching is fragile (overlay DB + lock files); marginal gain (~1–2 min) does not justify complexity. Revisit if wall time post-implementation is still a bottleneck — clean path then is pre-built base image on ghcr.io, not storage caching.

## Expected outcome

PR pipeline: ~4–6 min (from 12 min; below original 8 min baseline).

## Spike findings (retained for context)

- Disk: ~280MB extra for 7 roles; images persist after `molecule destroy` — periodic `podman image prune` needed
- Build time cold: 3–7 min serial; ~2 min with parallelism=4
- Build time warm: near-zero — layer cache reuses identical FROM + RUN
- Shared base image: rejected — breaks self-contained script contract, violates Principle IV
